Doing Major Time For a Minor Crime
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Every night around midnight, a dirty old van departs the Midtown South precinct and cruises lower Manhattan. This van will prowl through some of the nicest neighborhoods of the city and some of the worst. It will stay out all night, or until it is full. 

     The back of the van contains no seats, only a bare metal floor that smells faintly of vomit. As the evening progresses, this area will fill with members of every stratum of society, victims of a sweep by this old van and its convoy of unmarked escorts.

To Be 18 Years Old And Homeless
nytimes_articles.abstract:
I spent the first 21 years of my life in foster care in New York City. After  living with five foster families, I "graduated" from the system in 1991. Since then, I have also graduated from the State University of New York at Binghamton  and Columbia Law School, and have achieved the dream I nurtured since I was 10 years old. I am an advocate for children in foster care. 

     My sister, who spent 21 years mostly in group homes, began working in an entry-level position at a bank when she was 18. She stayed on after her discharge from foster care at 21 and is still there, supporting herself on a decent salary.

nytimes_articles.headline: The Black Truffle
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IF A BIOGRAPHY OF MY STOMACH WERE EVER TO BE written, the longest chapter would undoubtedly be a salute to the "divine tubercule" -- the lumpy, pungent, giddily expensive black truffle. 

      My passion for fresh truffles extends beyond the simple enjoyment of their taste, unique though that is. I love that they cannot be easily cultivated, and  that the most sophisticated equipment used in finding them is a dog's nose. I love the timing of the truffle season -- that otherwise fallow period between early January and late March. And, perhaps most of all, I love the whiff of villainy that hangs in the air whenever truffles are sold in the cafes and markets of the Vaucluse. There's something delightfully furtive about the transactions: sideways glances, winks and nods, grubby bank notes, no questions  asked -- and all you've done is buy the ingredients for an omelette de luxe.

SOAPBOX;700 Candid Words About Race
nytimes_articles.abstract:
FOR a time, Colin Powell's potential candidacy sharply altered the public discourse about race and politics. In reflection, it was all quite extraordinary: much of the leadership of America's white party, a k a the Republicans, pined to be led by the son of black immigrants. When he decided not to run for President, the hope that he alone could bridge the racial divide  was dashed. Now, the nation is left without a simplistic answer to its 375-year  racial quandary. We are back to tough, slow, messy choices. 

     While ads, sitcoms and movies portray an easy amiability between blacks and whites, there remain an awkwardness and artificiality when the subject brushes up against race. President Clinton, Senator Bill Bradley and others tell us that we should forge ahead, go out of our way to talk with neighbors, co-workers and  associates of other races -- and to do so with the intent of delving into otherwise taboo subjects like racial preferences in the workplace, black crime or family dissolution.

FILM;Film-Making Twins With a Taste for the Dark Side
nytimes_articles.abstract:
WHILE MANY identical twins try to set themselves apart from each other, Tim and Steven Quay seem to revel in their doubleness. The Brothers Quay, as they are jointly known, have worked together their entire lives, first as illustrators, then as animators celebrated for elaborately detailed puppet films shown in art houses and on MTV. On Wednesday, their first live-action feature, a black-and-white film called "Institute Benjimenta," has its premiere in the United States. 

     "Collaborating was always the most natural thing for us," said Stephen. The Brothers have made 18 films over the last 16 years. "You're reading the same literature, seeing the same films, seeing the same girls. You're that close."

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: SUNSET PARK;Awaiting Hong Kong Exodus
nytimes_articles.abstract:
On a recent rain-rattled afternoon, Shiu Kam Leung was walking down busy Eighth Avenue in Sunset Park, passing the prickly durians that hang in market doorways. For Mr. Leung, the bad-smelling yet sweet-tasting fruit popular in Asia is a harbinger of what he believes will be an economic boom in the neighborhood. 

     Mr. Leung, a business owner and president of an Eighth Avenue merchants association, left Hong Kong 15 years ago in anticipation of next year's government changeover. Today, he predicts that immigrants from Hong Kong will stream into the neighborhood in the next 500 days.

nytimes_articles.headline: Books in Brief: NONFICTION
Ambrose Bierce's End
Not Your Grandmother's Head Cheese
nytimes_articles.abstract:
FIVE YEARS AFTER MY grandmother's death, I wake knowing that never again will I taste her head cheese. You know what head cheese is, of course. Or were you not raised as I was, eating parts of animals that other people unreasonably despise? Head cheese (at least my grandmother's own peculiar recipe) is made from the meat boiled off the head of a pig or cow, all the chopped bits suspended in a vinegary-salty gelled broth. My grandmother hardened her head cheese in clean half-gallon Oak Grove milk cartons, so it was always sandwich size, square, studded with graceful curlicues of cartilage, sliced from the block as you unpeeled the waxed cardboard. I miss my grandmother's head cheese the way Proust missed his lime-blossom-tea-soaked madeleine cakes. I miss it more, because unlike his cakes, this head cheese is unavailable anywhere else and will never be tasted again upon this earth. 

      If, however, by some means, I did taste it again, the memories conjured would not be gentle. Even thinking of the taste of head cheese, I see the cow and see my uncle. The cow is black and white, a huge old Holstein awkward in stubborn terror. Pulled up the concrete ramp into the killing chute, where she will be pinned and shot, she balks. Her eyeballs slide back. Her bones strain through her hide. Her hooves strike the bars, clanging like a set of bells. She  resists, then she is in. The door slams down behind her. Now my uncle, a thick-muscled, red-haired man, scrambles up the side of the chute and balances himself, presses the barrel of the rifle against the skull of the cow. My cousins and I clap our hands over our ears. At the explosion, the cow rears massively, then sags, her skin mysteriously slack.

VOICES: FROM THE DESK OF;A Capitalist Camp in Siberia
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Given the international diversification of this company, its enviable record of increasing earnings and the quality of its products, we feel a premium multiple to that of the market makes some sense. But at a 22 multiple of current earnings and projected E.P.S. for 1995 of $2.25 a share, the current $53 price of International Flavors and Fragrances fully reflects its growth potential for  the next few years. We recommend investors defer purchase at this time." (May 20, 1995) An analyst's presentation to a group of mutual fund managers? A research report  culled from the pages of the Value Line Investment Survey? No, this is an evaluation by Max Bezyradin, a senior business major at Irkutsk State University in Irkutsk, Siberia, on his final examination in the finance course I taught there last spring. 

     Savvy investors, of course, are no strangers to the concept of international  diversification and the triumph of free-market ideology that has been sweeping the globe. Still, the idea of undergraduate students in Russia issuing investment advice -- well, even I was taken aback by the sophistication they had acquired so quickly.

TRAVEL ADVISORY;An Egyptian Museum Bites the Dust
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Demolition workers have begun pulling down the concrete framework of an unfinished museum that was to be constructed only 200 meters from the famous Zoser Step Pyramid near the Great Pyramids of Giza in Egypt. The decision came several weeks ago when the Egyptian Culture Minister, Farouq Hosni, toured the site and decided that the project posed an esthetic threat to the landscape of the area, 15 miles south of central Cairo on the west bank of the Nile. 

      Archeologists warned that the museum could also hinder further excavation at a potentially rich archeological area. A day before the minister ordered the destruction of the structure, a Late Ptolemaic sarcophagus and a clay coffin with a skeleton visible inside were discovered in the area. The museum -- the dream of the French Egyptologist Jean-Philippe Lauer, 93, who worked 65 years at Saqqara Plateau near the Pyramids -- was designed to house relics from the 4,700-year-old site and a model of Zoser.   EMAD MEKAY

What I've Learned From Toast
nytimes_articles.abstract:
NOT LONG AGO I WAS GIVEN A TOASTER AS A gift. For years I lived without one.  Toasting, it seemed to me, was a sham: in goes the pre-sliced slip of machine-molded white bread, and out pops a pretense of royalty, an imposter to the throne of cakes and jam and tea. The toaster -- invented, I'd always thought, expressly to facilitate this sorry masquerade -- was to blame. 

     But I've changed my views. Partly it's my toaster: chrome, sleek, a jewel from the 1950's -- that era when household appliances were an art form, when their design seemed to say, something sacred is taking place here. And partly it's a better understanding of history. Wonder Bread, the predecessor and king of all packaged sliced bread, was not introduced in sliced form until 1930, more than a decade after the world's first automatic toasting device (eventually called the Toastmaster toaster, the brand that now blesses my faux-wood-aminated counter) was patented. Sliced bread was literally made to be toasted.

How Far to Support Taiwan?
nytimes_articles.abstract:
By firing ballistic missiles within Taiwan's territorial waters, China is sending political and military messages to both the United States and Taiwan. Unless the Clinton Administration delivers a demonstrably tough response -- both diplomatically and militarily -- the exercises could escalate dangerously and Beijing will be convinced it can act with impunity. 

     The military exercises are but the latest in a long list of irritants, including Beijing's human rights violations and its sale of international arms.  The Clinton Administration has bent over backwards to engage China constructively and help it integrate into the world order.

Silence! Mr. Puddings in the Pot
nytimes_articles.abstract:
SATURDAY MORNINGS. The kitchen of a brownstone house in Brooklyn long decades ago. And my sister and I sitting in tense silence at the table, our prayerful gaze on the large pot on the stove. In it, simmering away, was Mr. Pudding, the  most highly prized dish from my mother's native Barbados. 

     Pudding consisted of about three feet of pig's belly that had been stuffed to bursting with a savory mix of grated raw sweet potatoes and beets, spices and fiery West Indian peppers to form a thick, delectable sausage.

WHAT'S DOING IN;San Francisco
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Never modest about their city, San Franciscans now have more reasons to boast. Instead of yet another Super Bowl victory by the Forty-Niners, however, civic pride is focusing on the new and revitalized arts institutions and museums that assure the city a position in the cultural big leagues. 

     In some cases, the 1989 earthquake was the catalyst for positive change, galvanizing community support for restoring buildings or for constructing new ones. The quake damaged the historic 1910 Geary Theater, leaving the city's repertory theater company, the American Conservatory Theater, without a permanent home for more than five years.  This January, freshly gilted, reinforced and technologically updated, the Geary reopened with an A.C.T. production of Shakespeare's "Tempest." On March 21, the Museum of the California Historical Society will officially open in Yerba Buena Gardens, where a new location for the first time provides space to exhibit from the society's extensive collections.

nytimes_articles.headline: A Bosnian Education
nytimes_articles.abstract: LOVE THY NEIGHBOR A Story of War. By Peter Maass. 305 pp. New York: Alfred A. Knopf. $25.     THE war in Bosnia has elicited no civilized response from the West other than good books written by journalists who have witnessed the carnage. No one knows this better than the journalists themselves, so their books are sad, verging on heartbroken, and Peter Maass's "Love Thy Neighbor: A Story of War" is the one of the saddest and best that I have read.

PLAYING IN THE NEIGHBORHOOD: GREENWICH VILLAGE;A Walk Into the Past Of a Countryside
Myrtle Beach Satisfies Golf Gluttons
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THE golf holiday, in its traditional middle-class male model, is an occasion  to binge. Escaping the long drive to a course and the long wait to tee off, and  saying goodbye to the family, the lawn and other demands on his leisure time, the golfer picks up his sticks and heads for a golf-vacation destination, where  the courses are plentiful and playing 27 to 36 holes a day for six days is virtually hassle-free. 

     This urge to, well, wallow in the sport -- to play from dawn to dusk and then start obsessing on plans for the next day's outing -- is now afflicting women, too, who are taking up golf in rapidly increasing numbers, especially businesswomen. The Executive Women's Golf Association, which started in 1991 with 28 members, now has more than 14,000 members in 90 chapters. According to the Ladies Professional Golf Association, about 38 percent of all beginners are  women.

A Life After I.B.M. Leads to Odd Sports Treasures
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THE search for new materials has led Philip L. Glazer to unusual finds. A stack of old football programs revealed a legendary 1934 Stanford-Columbia University Rose Bowlgame, the program autographed by the entire Columbia team. A tattered 1942 war bond program with autographs of Honus Wagner, Connie Mack and  George Sisler turned up at auction. 

     Mr. Glazer stocks so-called oddball sports collectibles, sports publications  and memorabilia in his business, Phil's Collectibles, here. But at least as unexpected as his wares is his background. Less than four years ago, he was developing marketing programs to sell supercomputers for International Business  Machines Corporation.

Health Teachers Now Are the Experts in Sex Education Class
nytimes_articles.abstract:
I REMEMBER the day in fifth grade when we had our first sex ed lesson," said  Jessica Moramarco, a ninth grader at Northport High School.  "They presented it  to the girls and boys separately, and everyone was sitting there red-faced, like, 'O.K., I want to go back to class now!' But because we've been talking about it in school ever since, now it's no big deal." 

     Jessica and three of her schoolmates were discussing their experiences as students of the Northport School District's comprehensive health and sex education program.  Since 1987, when the State Board of Education made AIDS education mandatory, public schools have had to develop age-appropriate curriculum for kindergarden through tweflth grade. The Northport district, which considers health and sex education a priority, is unique in that certified health teachers, not just classroom teachers, present the curriculum in elementary school. The staff felt that such specialists offer a continuity of instruction and are better trained to communicate sensitive subjects.

The View From: West Haven;Young Hands Helping Abandoned Animals
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THEY rush in, throw down their book bags, hang up their clothes, do their chores, then dash to their favorite pets. They're the Kennel Kids, who work with unwanted animals, helping out at the West Haven Animal Shelter: more particularly, the Judy E. Rettig Animal Care Facility. 

     On a winter day, Carissa Teta, 12, watered the dogs and cleaned cages. Sandra Tomlinson, 13, bathed a fluffy brown mixed bull terrier-labrador retriever. Caitlin Kelly, 12, hosed down a golden-hued lab, while Gina Montagna, 12, blow-dried a fluffy black puppy, one of a litter of 9. The girls also spent time talking to some traumatized dogs rescued from a burning home, helping them readjust to people.

VOICES: VIEWPOINT;Russia's Imbalance of Power
nytimes_articles.abstract:
ON June 16, Russia will hold its most important election of this century as its people vote to retain or replace President Boris N. Yeltsin. But this political puzzle, like all puzzles, has a clear, finished picture: No matter who is elected, Mr. Yeltsin will remain in power. The only question is what title he will hold -- or give himself. 

     I will not predict the mechanism by which Mr. Yeltsin will keep his power in  the framework of the Russian Constitution and political struggle. I can only make analogies. In China, there is a president, a prime minister, even a general secretary of the Communist Party, but power is held by Deng Xiaoping, whose only title now is President of the Bridge Association. Or one can go back to the 1920's in Poland, where Jozef Pilsudski, a hero of World War I, created the title Boss of the State after he seized power from the parliamentary Government.

The Prison Odyssey of Sol Wachtler
nytimes_articles.abstract:
In September 1993, Sol Wachtler, then 64, after resigning in disgrace as New  York State's Chief Judge, pleaded guilty and was sentenced to 15 months in Federal prison for harassing Joy Silverman, a woman with whom he had had an affair. This nationally prominent jurist was about to get an inmate's view of the American system of crime and punishment. Following are excerpts from his prison diary, which Mr. Wachtler intends to incorporate into a book. He kept the journal at the medium-security Federal Correctional Institution in Butner, N.C., and later at the Federal Medical Center in Rochester, Minn. At both prisons, Mr. Wachtler underwent treatment for manic depression, the disorder he  was diagnosed as having at the time of his crime. 

  Sept. 28, 1993 -- Day 1 I wish I had the gift of description -- the ability to find the words to describe my feelings this morning. As I was facing this apogee of my shame, I was hoping that there would be no press to greet me -- no pictures taken to feed the hungry maw of the tabloids -- to further embarrass my family and friends.

nytimes_articles.headline: Thatcher Urges Stronger Atlantic Alliance
Red, Hot and Chili
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THE HOTTEST THING IN my childhood was my father's temper. Our kitchen was empty of spices -- only some banal chaff in dusty bottles. We ate instant mashed potatoes, canned spinach, overcooked hash; food drained of color, growing cold.  

     When I left home and learned to cook, it was as though I'd never eaten before. I tried stuffing emerald green poblanos with cheese, threw slices of Anaheims green as grass into corn soup. I tried chicken mole. Spanish rice. Pasta puttanesca. I ate pico de gallo -- tomatoes, onions and diced Serranos; discovered chipotle, wicked smoked jalapenos canned in oil. A hungry lover taught me to start my kung pao in smoking oil crackling with chilies. Lately I've been making my own red curry paste, with a dozen chilies in every few cups, pureed with gingery kha, coarse lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, oil, sour tamarind. Poblanos are the spring of green; red curry is red at the heart of red, red's root.

March 3-9;Moscow, I Have A Problem
Approaching the Artichoke
nytimes_articles.abstract:
WHEN I WAS A TEEN-AGER LIVing in France with my family, I heard a story about artichokes that made a strong impression on me. My father met a woman whose well-to-do family had been forced, during World War II, to take in German soldiers. Naturally, her family had not been pleased at the prospect, and on the appointed day they planned their first move: a dish that would be a piece de resistance in the truest sense of the term. That night for dinner, they served artichokes, but -- under cover of food shortages -- they provided only enough for the soldiers. Non, non, il n'y a pas de quoi, they said. You go ahead and enjoy them. They are such a delicacy, n'est-ce pas? And then they sat back and watched in silence as the Nazis, utterly at a loss and with no one to imitate, choked and chewed through every leaf, thorn and bristle. 

     It was not so much the delicate violence that made this story lodge so firmly in my memory, or the fact that it represented such an utterly French form of revenge. Rather it was the way it showed the power of the small: as when a single bean grows into a giant beanstalk that leads to a castle, or a runaway dumpling links world and underworld, or a mere pea becomes the test of a genuine princess. Since I have never felt brave or physically strong, I have always known that any act of heroism or resistance on my own part would have to be accomplished through something as small as a bean or a dumpling and in just such a subtle, sly, artichoke sort of way.

For a Grucci, From Fireworks To Politics
nytimes_articles.abstract:
DRIVING through a snowstorm last month on his way to Brookhaven Town Hall, Felix J. Grucci Jr. described his move from pyrotechnics to politics. 

     In November, Mr. Grucci, chairman of Fireworks by Grucci, was elected as Supervisor with a 66 percent plurality, the largest margin in town history.

Paying for a Mistake
MUTUAL FUNDS;Those Limit Orders Have Their Limits
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THOUGH record amounts of cash have been pouring into mutual funds, some people have been sitting nervously on the sidelines, wanting to get in but wanting to avoid doing it at a market top. 

     Investors in individual stocks have always had an easy answer to such a quandary: They place a limit order with their broker. A limit order is an instruction to buy or sell a stock at a specified price. It allows an investor to request that a stock be bought at, say, 15 percent below today's market price, allowing the buyer to avoid the tip of an upward spike in the market.

Irish Coffee and Chocolate Sorbet
nytimes_articles.abstract: This recipe came to The Times in 1996 as part of a St. Patrick’s Day menu from James O’Shea, a restaurateur in Connecticut. It’s 20 minutes of prep time for an Irish-tinged treat any day of the year. To serve it after dinner, you’ll want to make sure the sorbet is in the freezer in the morning. Serve it in a tulip glass with plenty of whipped cream, and Mr. O’Shea likes Jameson’s for the whisky. Sláinte!
